data.title: The New Honeymoon: Romance, Adventure...Your Mom? — WSJ
data.description:
By Tara Weiss

When Deanne Peterson told friends she was going on a honeymoon, they were confused. The 74-year-old hadn't mentioned getting married.

Peterson explained she was accompanying her daughter and son-in-law on a two-week safari in Zambia and Zimbabwe.

An extended silence ensued.

It's not as unusual as you or Peterson's friends might think. Couples are inviting relatives on their postnuptial getaways, saying they have a lifetime for romance and solo travel but a finite period to spend with their families.

Not surprisingly, a "familymoon" can be a tough sell.

Peterson's daughter, Robin Dickson, said her friends were aghast when she told them about the plan to bring her mom. Friends of the groom chided him with mother-in-law jokes. They both insist the invitation felt natural.

"This chapter of life isn't just about starting our marriage, it's about honoring the people who shaped us," said Dickson, 35, of New York. "It felt like a celebration of family."

(The parents of the groom, Alex Dickson, were invited too but they declined, saying they would have come if the couple chose another location like the South of France.)

Bringing the in-laws is part of a broader redefining of the traditional honeymoon where newly marrieds cocoon themselves from the outside world. There's the buddymoon, in which newlyweds invite friends on vacation after the wedding. There's also the post-destination wedding extension.

Familymooners say the presence of mom and dad doesn't have to douse the flames.

Julia and Stefan Kuehn were brainstorming honeymoon locations when her parents, Eileen and Myron Petruch, mentioned they were planning an anniversary trip to South Africa and Mauritius. Eileen Petruch casually suggested they do a honeymoon-anniversary trip. Not wanting to be left out, Julia's younger sister joined too.

The couple, who live in Charleston, S.C., had gotten married during Covid in a ceremony with no guests, and had to postpone their honeymoon because international travel was tricky. So when her mom proposed the trip, they jumped at the idea, said Julia Kuehn, 30.

The extended clan embarked in the summer of 2023 for Africa, where they all agreed the best part of the trip was getting to see the Big Five game animals. The Kuehns carved out time for traditional honeymoon activities like a couples massage and in-suite private dinners. They also had their own villa in Mauritius with a private pool.

"There's a reason why Julia and I click so well and that's because we both value family," said Stefan Kuehn, 30. "It was an awesome trip and I would do it 10 times over."

Katie and Rayce Kopach divided their Italian honeymoon in two. First they spent a week sightseeing alone in Venice, Florence and Verona. Then they met up with Katie's parents in a villa in Tuscany where she said they cooked, decompressed and "made memories."

Katie, 34, crafted the plan after her father was diagnosed with a degenerative brain disorder three months before their 2017 wedding.

"We had to draw boundaries and be fulfilled before we met up with them," said Kopach, of Scottsdale, Ariz., whose parents flew in from Seattle. "The beginning of a marriage is usually focused on you and your partner but you can't choose when another huge life event happens so we had to adjust and make the best of the situation."

Some couples who ask friends and family to travel long distances to see them tie the knot are rewarding them with a wedding-honeymoon combo.

That's what Annie Morrison and Matt Kler did after their October 2022 nuptials in Scotland. When the wedding they were planning in Kalamazoo, Mich., was getting increasingly expensive, they texted about 30 members of their family asking if they'd be willing to travel overseas. Nineteen family and friends said yes so they scrapped the Michigan celebration for a destination wedding-honeymoon.

With their original plan of a large wedding, Morrison knew she'd want to be alone with her new husband on a honeymoon. Once they changed the format, her thinking changed. "Why wouldn't we want to travel together? We love you guys," Morrison, 29, of Summerville, S.C., said.

They surveyed their guests about where they'd like to visit and prepared an itinerary of their mini tour of Scotland based on the results.

"Matt's dad said it was the best vacation he's ever been on," Morrison said.

When their family and friends left at the end of the week, Morrison and Kler rented a luxurious hotel room in Edinburgh and stayed an extra day.

Kler, 30, admits, "It was nice to be Annie and I for a day."

As for the Dicksons, the safari with mom was a success.

The three traveled to Zambia, where they stayed in a five-star resort. Many days were spent on daylong game drives to view wildlife. The couple stayed in a suite at the opposite end of the resort from Peterson, and set aside some alone time, taking a riverboat expedition and a jeep tour in the African bush that included a "magical, massive" brunch prepared by a private chef.

Many of their fellow guests at the resort were parents of adult children who remarked that they couldn't imagine their own children inviting them or their in-laws on their honeymoon.

"We 100% had our own honeymoon but my mom happened to be there sometimes, " said Robin Dickson.

data.title: REG - Magyar Fejlesztsi Bk - Capital increase in MFB Invest Plc.
data.description:
RNS Number : 6234P MFB Magyar Fejlesztési Bank 03 July 2025  

Announcement

on capital increase in MFB Invest Plc.

In accordance with the extraordinary disclosure obligation stipulated under Section 55 of (Hungarian) Act CXX of 2001 on the Capital Market and PM (Minister of Finance) Decree No. 24/2008 (VIII.15.), MFB Hungarian Development Bank Private Limited Company (hereinafter: MFB Zrt.) informs the public of the following:

According to the resolution No. 30/2025. (VII.1.) issued by the General Meeting of MFB Invest Plc., MFB Zrt. - in order to finance the business activity - will increase the share capital of MFB Invest Plc. by HUF 161,000,000,000 (one hundred and sixty-one billion Hungarian forints) to be paid by cash contribution.

Budapest, 3 July 2025

MFB Hungarian Development Bank Private Limited Company

JPMorgan Names David Frame as Global Head of Private Bank — WSJ
Airtac International Logs Higher Revenue in June
data.description:
Airtac International booked consolidated revenue of 656.3 million yuan for June, up 16% versus a year prior.

In local currency, the revenue rose 7% to NT$2.7 billion, according to a Thursday Taiwan Exchange filing.

For the first half of 2025, revenue expanded 10% to 3.9 billion yuan, or up 9% to NT$17 billion, it said.

The equipment manufacturer reports in yuan and converts results to New Taiwan Dollars at the exchange rate used.

data.title: Singapore Sustains Private-Sector Expansion in June: PMI Report
data.description:
Singapore's private-sector boosted output, and enterprise confidence rose in June, reported S&P Global on Thursday.

Singapore's purchasing managers index (PMI) logged at 51.0 in June, down from 51.5 in May, but still striking above the 50-mark that separates growth from contraction, reported S&P Global, citing its monthly survey.

Unlike for most other nations or regions, for the city-state of Singapore S&P Global publishes only a single composite PMI, for both manufacturing and service sectors.

June marked "the fifth consecutive month" the PMI posted above the 50-mark, as Singapore business managers reported higher new work inflows, said S&P Global.

However, Singapore business continued to trim payrolls in June, despite rising orders and backlogs. "Concurrently, staffing levels fell (in June) for the seventh month in a row, attributed to the non-replacement of job leavers and shedding of part-time staff. Outstanding business accumulated as a result of the reduction in workforce capacity," said S&P Global.

Singapore business managers reported muted inflation in June, and well as modest increases in prices charged.

"Turning to prices, average input costs increased at the softest pace...over five years," reported S&P Global. "This was while wage costs continued to fall."

Singapore businesses left average charges unchanged in June. "Singaporean firms indicated that they opted to absorb cost increases to support sales amid heightened competition," explained S&P Global.

In June, the optimism of Singapore business managers improved, but from a low level.

Sentiment "in the Singaporean private sector improved at the end of the second quarter. The level of confidence was below the long-run average, but nevertheless represented an improvement from outright pessimism in May," advised S&P Global. "Businesses were hopeful that expansion plans and supportive policies will support output growth in the year ahead."

The June Singapore PMI was compiled by S&P Global from surveys sent to 400 private-sector companies from June 12 through June 24.

data.title: Tariff deadline likely a "non-event" for G10 FX, but watch Japan - Citi
data.description:
Key points:
  • STOXX 600 up 0.3%
  • House to vote on Trump's tax bill
  • Eyes on payrolls
  • U.S. futures higher

TARIFF DEADLINE LIKELY A "NON-EVENT" FOR G10 FX, BUT WATCH JAPAN - CITI

Calm has returned to markets since the 90-day pause that U.S. President Donald Trump activated following the chaos unleashed by his April 2 "Liberation Day" tariff announcement and while no one knows the next move, Citi FX strategists appear relaxed.

They believe, for the most part, the upcoming July 9 tariff deadline is likely to be a "non-event" for G10 FX although risks remain should the European Union not strike an extension.

"Expect lots of headline noise, but also actual deals into July 9," Citi says.

Their main focuses in the G10 are the European Union and Japan.

On the EU, Citi's base case is that the two agree a framework of a deal by July 9, which should see the 10% tariff rate extended and negotiations continue.

"Given recent EUR strength, we suspect such news will be marginally EUR positive, but not necessarily a significant driver as a lot of good news is already priced for the currency."

On Japan, Citi thinks the potential for a deal is decreasing given Trump's recent comments.

"We've dealt with Japan. I'm not sure we're going to make a deal. I doubt it," Trump said this week.

"Risks of an increased tariff rate appear highest in Japan," Citi says, adding that there could be some yen weakness in the coming weeks.

"An increased concern for higher tariffs only adds to existing pessimism which could weigh on Japanese markets," they say.

The bank sees USD/JPY climbing to 150 this summer before dropping below 140 later this year as yen strength returns on expected policy normalisation from the Bank of Japan.

(Samuel Indyk)

*****

THURSDAY'S OTHER LIVE MARKETS POSTS:

STOXX TICKS HIGHER CLICK HERE

EUROPE BEFORE THE BELL: FUTURES INCH HIGHER BEFORE PAYROLLS CLICK HERE

TRADE OPTIMISM GIVES WAY TO CAUTION OVER US JOBS CLICK HERE

data.title: UK's Railway Regulator Turns Down New Service Applications for West Coast Main Line
data.description:
The UK's Office of Rail and Road said Thursday it rejected applications from three operators to introduce new services on the West Coast Main Line, citing insufficient capacity on the southern section of the route.

The regulator turned down track access requests from East Coast Trains, the Wrexham, Shropshire & Midlands Railway Co., and Virgin Management following a review of the applicants' operational viability, potential benefits, and feedback from passengers and local stakeholders.

data.title: Mkango Resources' Unit Signs Definitive Merger Deal With Crown PropTech
data.description:
Mkango Resources said Thursday its unit Lancaster Exploration signed a definitive business combination agreement with Crown PropTech to form Mkango Rare Earths.

Mkango will retain a majority stake in the new entity post-merger with a pro forma equity value of $400 million. The deal completion is subject to shareholder and regulatory approvals.

The merged entity, a vertically integrated rare earths company, is expected to be listed on US' Nasdaq and will own the Songwe Hill and Pulawy projects in Poland.

Notes

i. Persons completing this form should have regard to the requirements of the Transparency (Directive 2004/109/EC) Regulations 2007 as amended (the "Regulations"), the Central Bank of Ireland's Transparency Rules (the "Transparency Rules") and Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2015/761 of 17 December 2014.

ii Full name of the legal entity and other identifying specification of the issuer or underlying issuer, provided it is reliable and accurate (e.g. address, LEI, domestic number identity).

iii Other reason for the notification could be voluntary notifications, changes of attribution of the nature of the holding (e.g. expiring of financial instruments) or acting in concert.

iv This should be the full name of (a) the shareholder; (b) the natural person or legal entity acquiring, disposing of or exercising voting rights in the cases provided for in Regulation 15(b) to (h) of the Regulations (Article 10 (b) to (h) of Directive 2004/109/EC); or (c) the holder of financial instruments referred to in Regulation 17(1) of the Regulations (Article 13(1) of Directive 2004/109/EC).

As the disclosure of cases of acting in concert may vary due to the specific circumstances (e.g. same or different total positions of the parties, entering or exiting of acting in concert by a single party) the standard form does not provide for a specific method how to notify cases of acting in concert.

In relation to the transactions referred to in points (b) to (h) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the following list is provided as an indication of the persons who should be mentioned:

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (b) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the natural person or legal entity that acquires the voting rights and is entitled to exercise them under the agreement and the natural person or legal entity who is transferring temporarily for consideration the voting rights;

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (c) of the Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the natural person or legal entity holding the collateral, provided the person or entity controls the voting rights and declares its intention of exercising them, and natural person or legal entity lodging the collateral under these conditions;

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (d) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the natural person or legal entity who has a life interest in shares if that person or entity is entitled to exercise the voting rights attached to the shares and the natural person or legal entity who is disposing of the voting rights when the life interest is created;

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (e) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the controlling natural person or legal entity and, provided it has a notification duty at an individual level under Regulation 14 of the Regulations (Article 9 of Directive 2004/109/EC), under letters (a) to (d) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC) or under a combination of any of those situations, the controlled undertaking;

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (f) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the deposit taker of the shares, if he can exercise the voting rights attached to the shares deposited with him at his discretion, and the depositor of the shares allowing the deposit taker to exercise the voting rights at his discretion;

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (g) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the natural person or legal entity that controls the voting rights;

-     in the circumstances foreseen in letter (h) of Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 of Directive 2004/109/EC), the proxy holder, if he can exercise the voting rights at his discretion, and the shareholder who has given his proxy to the proxy holder allowing the latter to exercise the voting rights at his discretion (e.g. management companies).

v Applicable in the cases provided for in Regulation 15(b) to (h) of the Regulations (Article 10 (b) to (h) of Directive 2004/109/EC). This should be the full name of the shareholder who is the counterparty to the natural person or legal entity referred to in Regulation 15 of the Regulations (Article 10 Directive 2004/109/EC) unless the percentage of voting rights held by the shareholder is lower than the lowest notifiable threshold for the disclosure of voting rights holdings in accordance with the requirements of the Regulations and the Transparency Rules.

vi The date on which threshold is crossed or reached should be the date on which the acquisition or disposal took place or the other reason triggered the notification obligation. For passive crossings, the date when the corporate event took effect.

vii The total number of voting rights shall be composed of all the shares, including depository receipts representing shares, to which voting rights are attached even if the exercise thereof is suspended.

viii If the holding has fallen below the lowest applicable threshold in accordance with the Regulations and the Transparency Rules  the holder is not obliged to disclose the extent of the holding only that the holding is "below 3%" or "below 5%" as appropriate.

ix In case of combined holdings of shares with voting rights attached "direct holding" and voting rights "indirect holding", please split the voting rights number and percentage into the direct and indirect columns - if there is no combined holdings, please leave the relevant box blank.

x Date of maturity/expiration of the financial instrument i.e. the date when right to acquire shares ends.

xi If the financial instrument has such a period - please specify this period - for example once every 3 months starting from [date].

xii In case of cash settled instruments the number and percentages of voting rights is to be presented on a delta-adjusted basis (Regulation 17(4) of the Regulations/Article 13(1a) of Directive 2004/109/EC).

xiii If the person subject to the notification obligation is either controlled and/or does control another undertaking then the second option applies.

xiv The full chain of controlled undertakings, starting with the ultimate controlling natural person or legal entity, has to be presented also in cases in which only on subsidiary level a threshold is crossed or reached and the subsidiary undertaking discloses the notification, as only thus will the markets get a full picture of the group holdings. In the case of multiple chains through which the voting rights and/or financial instruments are effectively held, the chains have to be presented chain by chain leaving a row free between different chains (e.g.: A, B, C, free row, A, B, D, free row, A, E, F etc.).

xv The names of controlled undertakings through which the voting rights and/or financial instruments are effectively held have to be presented irrespective of whether the controlled undertakings cross or reach the lowest applicable threshold themselves.

xvi Example: Correction of a previous notification.

This information is provided by RNS, the news service of the London Stock Exchange. RNS is approved by the Financial Conduct Authority to act as a Primary Information Provider in the United Kingdom. Terms and conditions relating to the use and distribution of this information may apply. For further information, please contact rns@lseg.com or visit www.rns.com.

RNS may use your IP address to confirm compliance with the terms and conditions, to analyse how you engage with the information contained in this communication, and to share such analysis on an anonymised basis with others as part of our commercial services. For further information about how RNS and the London Stock Exchange use the personal data you provide us, please see our Privacy Policy.  END  ISEUOUKRVOUBRRR

    e.g. subscription, conversion

  • Price per unit (if applicable)

3.         OTHER INFORMATION

(a)        Indemnity and other dealing arrangements

  • Details of any indemnity or option arrangement, or any agreement or understanding, formal or informal, relating to relevant securities which may be an inducement to deal or refrain from dealing entered into by the exempt principal trader making the disclosure and any party to the offer or any person acting in concert with a party to the offer:

    Irrevocable commitments and letters of intent should not be included.  If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • NONE

(b)        Agreements, arrangements or understandings relating to options or derivatives

  • Details of any agreement, arrangement or understanding, formal or informal, between the exempt principal trader making the disclosure and any other person relating to:

    (i)  the voting rights of any relevant securities under any option; or

    (ii) the voting rights or future acquisition or disposal of any relevant securities to which any derivative is referenced:

    If there are no such agreements, arrangements or understandings, state "none"

  • NONE

Date of disclosure:

03/07/2025

Contact name:

Rosie Kinsella

Telephone number:

+44 (0)207 886 2574

Public disclosures under Rule 8 of the Code must be made to a Regulatory Information Service. 

The Panel's Market Surveillance Unit is available for consultation in relation to the Code's dealing disclosure requirements on +44 (0)20 7638 0129.

The Code can be viewed on the Panel's website at www.thetakeoverpanel.org.uk.

data.title: Aker ASA: Presentation of Second-Quarter and Half-Year Results 2025
data.description:
Aker ASA

Aker ASA will publish its financial results for the second quarter and half-year of 2025 on July 16, 2025, at 07:00 CEST. An online webcast presentation of the results will be available the same day at 09:00 CEST.

Webcast details:

Date: Wednesday, July 16, 2025

Time: 09:00am CEST

Format: Webcast

Language: English

Link: www.akerasa.com/webcast

There will be a prepared Q&A session following the presentation. It is possible to submit questions to fredrik.berge@akerasa.com in advance

The presentation and material will be made available at www.akerasa.com and www.newsweb.no.

ENDS

Investor contact:

Fredrik Berge, Head of Investor Relations

Tel: +47 45 03 20 90

E-mail: fredrik.berge@akerasa.com

Media contact:

Atle Kigen, Head of Media Relations and Public Affairs

Tel: +47 90 78 48 78

E-mail: atle.kigen@akerasa.com

This information is subject to the disclosure requirements pursuant to Section 5-12 the Norwegian Securities Trading Act

https://news.cision.com/aker-asa/r/aker-asa--presentation-of-second-quarter-and-half-year-results-2025%2Cc4183793
